title: Floquet engineering of nonreciprocal light-induced dipolar interactions
authors: Livia Egyed, Murad Abuzarli, Manuel Reisenbauer, Iurie Coroli, Benjamin A. Stickler, Uroš Delić
abstract: Tweezer arrays of polarizable objects are a promising platform for assembling quantum matter and building next-generation quantum sensors. Light-induced dipolar interactions have emerged as a method to couple their motion, thereby establishing a new paradigm for controlling collective mechanical degrees of freedom. Here, we extend these into the regime of Floquet-driven interactions, combined with the intrinsic nonreciprocity of optical forces. We demonstrate beamsplitter, single-, and two-mode squeezing operations, as well as signatures of a negative-mass-like oscillator arising from the nonreciprocity. Moreover, we show that a programmable combination of these operations enables continuous tuning of complex eigenfrequencies. These results establish a toolbox of quantum operations of nonreciprocal interactions that are essential for investigating non-Hermitian many-body physics and collective quantum optomechanics.
summary: The researchers use Floquet engineering to control light-induced interactions between trapped nanoparticles, enabling the creation of programmable quantum operations like squeezing and beamsplitters. This work establishes a platform for exploring non-reciprocal dynamics and non-Hermitian many-body physics.
main_problem: Achieving programmable, time-dependent control over light-induced dipolar interactions between nanoparticles to realize fundamental quantum optical operations like beamsplitters and squeezing.
main_result: Demonstrated full control over beamsplitter, single-mode, and two-mode squeezing operations, as well as the realization of non-reciprocal dynamics and negative-mass-like oscillator signatures.
method: Floquet engineering via optical detuning of acousto-optic driven tweezers, combined with heterodyne detection and Hilbert transform-based reconstruction of eigenmodes.
model_or_system: Two silica nanoparticles trapped in separate optical tweezers within a vacuum chamber, interacting via light-induced dipolar forces (optical binding) modulated by optical frequency detuning.
key_observables: Power spectral densities (PSD), particle positions and momenta, mechanical sidebands, quadrature variances, and first/second-order correlation functions.
important_parameters: Optical detuning (delta omega), interparticle distance (kd), mechanical frequencies (Omega), coupling rate (g), and damping rate (gamma).
main_assumptions: Rotating-wave approximation (RWA) for resonant interactions, high-Q oscillator assumption, and Gaussian statistics for thermal noise.
figures_summary: Spectrograms of particle positions and mode eigenfrequencies; plots of coherent energy exchange and squeezing magnitudes; and phase-space plots illustrating single-mode squeezing and bistability.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of programmable interactions, describes the experimental setup with AOD-driven tweezers, derives the time-dependent Floquet-driven Hamiltonian, demonstrates various squeezing and beamsplitter regimes, and concludes with potential applications in non-Hermitian physics.
why_interesting: It provides an experimental toolbox for engineering non-reciprocal and non-Hermitian interactions, which is highly relevant for studying exceptional points, reservoir engineering, and topological phases in open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Time-dependent light-induced dipolar inter- actions. a) Two particles with mechanical frequencies Ω1,2 in tweezers with different optical frequencies ω and ω + ∆ω interact via scattered light, which forms a phase-modulated standing wave and locally changes trapping potentials. The optical detuning yields time-dependent optical forces, so that the reciprocal coupling shown here changes from maximally positive to negative coupling rates after a time π/∆ω. b) Time-modulated directional coupling rates g12 and g21 be- tween the particles. Depending on the interparticle distance, the interaction can be reciprocal (blue), anti-reciprocal (or- ange), or their combination (gray). The...

FIG. 2. Spectrograms of single- and two-mode opera- tions. Combined spectrograms of the motion z1 and z2 with frequencies Ω1/2π ≈27 kHz and Ω2/2π ≈33 kHz reveal interactions at different resonances: a) reciprocal beamsplit- ter at ∆ω/2π ≈6 kHz, b) reciprocal two-mode squeezing at ∆ω/2π ≈60 kHz, c) anti-reciprocal two-mode squeezing at ∆ω/2π ≈6 kHz, d) anti-reciprocal beamsplitter interaction at ∆ω/2π ≈60 kHz. Single-mode squeezing is observed in b) and d) at ∆ω ≈2Ω1,2. The black lines are fits based on the theoretical dependence of the normal mode frequencies.

FIG. 3. Programmable two-mode operations for reciprocal and anti-reciprocal interactions. a) Feedback cooling on the charged particle is switched off, while the beamsplitter interaction is switched on at time t = 0 ms. Coherent energy exchange between reciprocally (top) and anti-reciprocally (bottom) coupled charged (green) and uncharged (red) particles measured at optical detunings ∆ω ≈∆Ωand ∆ω ≈2¯Ω, respectively, and averaged over around 1000 realizations. Dashed lines are theoretical fits. b) Stationary two-mode thermomechanical noise squashing between reciprocally coupled particles (top) shows correlations (anti-correlations) of scaled positions ˜z1,2 (momenta ˜p1,2). For...

FIG. 4. Tuning eigenfrequencies in the complex plane. a) The difference between the real part of the normal mode frequencies (blue) and imaginary part (orange) as a function of the scaled interparticle distance kd. The lines are a fit to the theory. The error bars are a combination of errors of the distance, deviations of ∆ω from ∆Ω, and the fit errors. b) The reconstructed phase space of complex eigenfrequencies shows continuous tuning of both the real and imaginary parts that traces a circle around the origin, as predicted by theory (gray line).
